packages.name: chi
packages.synopsis: Package chi is a small, idiomatic and composable router for building HTTP services.
packages.doc:
Package chi is a small, idiomatic and composable router for building HTTP services.

chi supports the four most recent major versions of Go.

Example:

	package main

	import (
		"net/http"

		"github.com/go-chi/chi/v5"
		"github.com/go-chi/chi/v5/middleware"
	)

	func main() {
		r := chi.NewRouter()
		r.Use(middleware.Logger)
		r.Use(middleware.Recoverer)

		r.Get("/", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
			w.Write([]byte("root."))
		})

		http.ListenAndServe(":3333", r)
	}

See github.com/go-chi/chi/_examples/ for more in-depth examples.

URL patterns allow for easy matching of path components in HTTP
requests. The matching components can then be accessed using
chi.URLParam(). All patterns must begin with a slash.

A simple named placeholder {name} matches any sequence of characters
up to the next / or the end of the URL. Trailing slashes on paths must
be handled explicitly.

A placeholder with a name followed by a colon allows a regular
expression match, for example {number:\\d+}. The regular expression
syntax is Go's normal regexp RE2 syntax, except that / will never be
matched. An anonymous regexp pattern is allowed, using an empty string
before the colon in the placeholder, such as {:\\d+}

The special placeholder of asterisk matches the rest of the requested
URL. Any trailing characters in the pattern are ignored. This is the only
placeholder which will match / characters.

Examples:

	"/user/{name}" matches "/user/jsmith" but not "/user/jsmith/info" or "/user/jsmith/"
	"/user/{name}/info" matches "/user/jsmith/info"
	"/page/*" matches "/page/intro/latest"
	"/page/{other}/latest" also matches "/page/intro/latest"
	"/date/{yyyy:\\d\\d\\d\\d}/{mm:\\d\\d}/{dd:\\d\\d}" matches "/date/2017/04/01"

vars.name: RouteCtxKey

funcs.name: Chain
funcs.doc: Chain returns a Middlewares type from a slice of middleware handlers.

funcs.name: NewMux
funcs.doc:
NewMux returns a newly initialized Mux object that implements the Router
interface.

funcs.name: NewRouteContext
funcs.doc: NewRouteContext returns a new routing Context object.

funcs.name: NewRouter
funcs.doc: NewRouter returns a new Mux object that implements the Router interface.

funcs.name: RegisterMethod
funcs.doc:
RegisterMethod adds support for custom HTTP method handlers, available
via Router#Method and Router#MethodFunc

funcs.name: RouteContext
funcs.doc:
RouteContext returns chi's routing Context object from a
http.Request Context.

funcs.name: URLParam
funcs.doc: URLParam returns the url parameter from a http.Request object.

funcs.name: URLParamFromCtx
funcs.doc: URLParamFromCtx returns the url parameter from a http.Request Context.
funcs.signature: func URLParamFromCtx(ctx context.Context, key string) string

funcs.name: Walk
funcs.doc: Walk walks any router tree that implements Routes interface.
funcs.signature: func Walk(r Routes, walkFn WalkFunc) error

types.name: ChainHandler
types.doc:
ChainHandler is a http.Handler with support for handler composition and
execution.

types.name: Context
types.doc:
Context is the default routing context set on the root node of a
request context to track route patterns, URL parameters and
an optional routing path.

fields.name: RoutePath
fields.doc:
Routing path/method override used during the route search.
See Mux#routeHTTP method.

fields.name: URLParams
fields.doc:
URLParams are the stack of routeParams captured during the
routing lifecycle across a stack of sub-routers.

fields.name: RoutePatterns
fields.doc:
Routing pattern stack throughout the lifecycle of the request,
across all connected routers. It is a record of all matching
patterns across a stack of sub-routers.

methods.name: Reset
methods.doc: Reset a routing context to its initial state.

methods.name: RoutePattern
URLParam
methods.doc:
URLParam returns the corresponding URL parameter value from the request
routing context.

types.name: Middlewares
types.doc:
Middlewares type is a slice of standard middleware handlers with methods
to compose middleware chains and http.Handler's.
types.declaration: type Middlewares []func(http.Handler) http.Handler

methods.name: Handler
methods.doc:
Handler builds and returns a http.Handler from the chain of middlewares,
with `h http.Handler` as the final handler.

methods.name: HandlerFunc
methods.doc:
HandlerFunc builds and returns a http.Handler from the chain of middlewares,
with `h http.Handler` as the final handler.

types.name: Mux
types.doc:
Mux is a simple HTTP route multiplexer that parses a request path,
records any URL params, and executes an end handler. It implements
the http.Handler interface and is friendly with the standard library.

Mux is designed to be fast, minimal and offer a powerful API for building
modular and composable HTTP services with a large set of handlers. It's
particularly useful for writing large REST API services that break a handler
into many smaller parts composed of middlewares and end handlers.

methods.name: Connect
methods.doc:
Connect adds the route `pattern` that matches a CONNECT http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Delete
methods.doc:
Delete adds the route `pattern` that matches a DELETE http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Find
methods.doc:
Find searches the routing tree for the pattern that matches
the method/path.

Note: the *Context state is updated during execution, so manage
the state carefully or make a NewRouteContext().
methods.signature: func  Find(rctx *Context, method, path string) string

methods.name: Get
methods.doc:
Get adds the route `pattern` that matches a GET http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Group
methods.doc:
Group creates a new inline-Mux with a copy of middleware stack. It's useful
for a group of handlers along the same routing path that use an additional
set of middlewares. See _examples/.

methods.name: Handle
methods.doc:
Handle adds the route `pattern` that matches any http method to
execute the `handler` http.Handler.

methods.name: HandleFunc
methods.doc:
HandleFunc adds the route `pattern` that matches any http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Head
methods.doc:
Head adds the route `pattern` that matches a HEAD http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Match
methods.doc:
Match searches the routing tree for a handler that matches the method/path.
It's similar to routing a http request, but without executing the handler
thereafter.

Note: the *Context state is updated during execution, so manage
the state carefully or make a NewRouteContext().
methods.signature: func  Match(rctx *Context, method, path string) bool

methods.name: Method
methods.doc:
Method adds the route `pattern` that matches `method` http method to
execute the `handler` http.Handler.
methods.signature: func  Method(method, pattern string, handler http.Handler)

methods.name: MethodFunc
methods.doc:
MethodFunc adds the route `pattern` that matches `method` http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.
methods.signature: func  MethodFunc(method, pattern string, handlerFn http.HandlerFunc)

methods.name: MethodNotAllowed
methods.doc:
MethodNotAllowed sets a custom http.HandlerFunc for routing paths where the
method is unresolved. The default handler returns a 405 with an empty body.

methods.name: MethodNotAllowedHandler
methods.doc:
MethodNotAllowedHandler returns the default Mux 405 responder whenever
a method cannot be resolved for a route.

methods.name: Middlewares
methods.doc: Middlewares returns a slice of middleware handler functions.

methods.name: Mount
methods.doc:
Mount attaches another http.Handler or chi Router as a subrouter along a routing
path. It's very useful to split up a large API as many independent routers and
compose them as a single service using Mount. See _examples/.

Note that Mount() simply sets a wildcard along the `pattern` that will continue
routing at the `handler`, which in most cases is another chi.Router. As a result,
if you define two Mount() routes on the exact same pattern the mount will panic.

methods.name: NotFound
methods.doc:
NotFound sets a custom http.HandlerFunc for routing paths that could
not be found. The default 404 handler is `http.NotFound`.

methods.name: NotFoundHandler
methods.doc:
NotFoundHandler returns the default Mux 404 responder whenever a route
cannot be found.

methods.name: Options
methods.doc:
Options adds the route `pattern` that matches an OPTIONS http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Patch
methods.doc:
Patch adds the route `pattern` that matches a PATCH http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Post
methods.doc:
Post adds the route `pattern` that matches a POST http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Put
methods.doc:
Put adds the route `pattern` that matches a PUT http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Route
methods.doc:
Route creates a new Mux and mounts it along the `pattern` as a subrouter.
Effectively, this is a short-hand call to Mount. See _examples/.
methods.signature: func  Route(pattern string, fn func(r Router)) Router

methods.name: Routes
methods.doc:
Routes returns a slice of routing information from the tree,
useful for traversing available routes of a router.

methods.name: ServeHTTP
methods.doc:
ServeHTTP is the single method of the http.Handler interface that makes
Mux interoperable with the standard library. It uses a sync.Pool to get and
reuse routing contexts for each request.

methods.name: Trace
methods.doc:
Trace adds the route `pattern` that matches a TRACE http method to
execute the `handlerFn` http.HandlerFunc.

methods.name: Use
methods.doc:
Use appends a middleware handler to the Mux middleware stack.

The middleware stack for any Mux will execute before searching for a matching
route to a specific handler, which provides opportunity to respond early,
change the course of the request execution, or set request-scoped values for
the next http.Handler.

methods.name: With
methods.doc: With adds inline middlewares for an endpoint handler.

types.name: Route
types.doc:
Route describes the details of a routing handler.
Handlers map key is an HTTP method

types.name: RouteParams
types.doc: RouteParams is a structure to track URL routing parameters efficiently.

methods.name: Add
methods.doc: Add will append a URL parameter to the end of the route param

types.name: Router
types.doc:
Router consisting of the core routing methods used by chi's Mux,
using only the standard net/http.

fields.name: Use
fields.doc: Use appends one or more middlewares onto the Router stack.

fields.name: With
fields.doc: With adds inline middlewares for an endpoint handler.

fields.name: Group
fields.doc:
Group adds a new inline-Router along the current routing
path, with a fresh middleware stack for the inline-Router.

fields.name: Route
fields.doc: Route mounts a sub-Router along a `pattern` string.
fields.type: func(pattern string, fn func(r Router)) Router

fields.name: Mount
fields.doc: Mount attaches another http.Handler along ./pattern/*

fields.name: Handle
fields.doc:
Handle and HandleFunc adds routes for `pattern` that matches
all HTTP methods.

fields.name: Method
fields.doc:
Method and MethodFunc adds routes for `pattern` that matches
the `method` HTTP method.

fields.name: Connect
fields.doc: HTTP-method routing along `pattern`

fields.name: NotFound
fields.doc:
NotFound defines a handler to respond whenever a route could
not be found.

fields.name: MethodNotAllowed
fields.doc:
MethodNotAllowed defines a handler to respond whenever a method is
not allowed.

types.name: Routes
types.doc:
Routes interface adds two methods for router traversal, which is also
used by the `docgen` subpackage to generation documentation for Routers.

fields.name: Routes
fields.doc: Routes returns the routing tree in an easily traversable structure.

fields.name: Middlewares
fields.doc: Middlewares returns the list of middlewares in use by the router.

fields.name: Match
fields.doc:
Match searches the routing tree for a handler that matches
the method/path - similar to routing a http request, but without
executing the handler thereafter.

fields.name: Find
fields.doc:
Find searches the routing tree for the pattern that matches
the method/path.

types.name: WalkFunc
types.doc: WalkFunc is the type of the function called for each method and route visited by Walk.
types.declaration: type WalkFunc func(method string, route string, handler http.Handler, middlewares ...func(http.Handler) http.Handler) error

consts.name: RequestIDKey
consts.doc: RequestIDKey is the key that holds the unique request ID in a request context.

vars.name: LogEntryCtxKey
DefaultLogger
RequestIDHeader
vars.doc:
RequestIDHeader is the name of the HTTP Header which contains the request id.
Exported so that it can be changed by developers

vars.name: URLFormatCtxKey

funcs.name: AllowContentEncoding
funcs.doc:
AllowContentEncoding enforces a whitelist of request Content-Encoding otherwise responds
with a 415 Unsupported Media Type status.

funcs.name: AllowContentType
funcs.doc:
AllowContentType enforces a whitelist of request Content-Types otherwise responds
with a 415 Unsupported Media Type status.

funcs.name: BasicAuth
funcs.doc: BasicAuth implements a simple middleware handler for adding basic http auth to a route.
funcs.signature: func BasicAuth(realm string, creds map[string]string) func(next http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: CleanPath
funcs.doc:
CleanPath middleware will clean out double slash mistakes from a user's request path.
For example, if a user requests /users//1 or //users////1 will both be treated as: /users/1

funcs.name: ClientIPFromHeader
funcs.doc:
ClientIPFromHeader stores the client IP from a single-IP header set by
your reverse proxy. Read it with [GetClientIP].

Only safe with headers your proxy unconditionally OVERWRITES on every
request, e.g.:

  - X-Real-IP        — Nginx with ngx_http_realip_module
  - X-Client-IP      — Apache with mod_remoteip
  - CF-Connecting-IP — Cloudflare

True-Client-IP, X-Azure-ClientIP, and Fastly-Client-IP look similar but
pass through from the client by default in those products; don't use them
unless your edge strips the inbound value.

If the header reaches us with multiple values (misconfigured proxy that
appends, or a downstream proxy not stripping a client-supplied value),
the LAST value wins — that's the one set by the hop closest to us, and
therefore the most trusted. Fail-closed if the last value doesn't parse:
no client IP is set rather than falling back to earlier (less-trusted)
values.

v4-mapped IPv6 (::ffff:a.b.c.d) folds to plain v4 and IPv6 zones are
stripped before storage.

funcs.name: ClientIPFromRemoteAddr
funcs.doc:
ClientIPFromRemoteAddr stores the client IP read from the TCP RemoteAddr
of the incoming request — the IP address of whoever opened the connection
to this server. Read it with [GetClientIP].

Use this when this server is directly connected to the public internet
with NO reverse proxy in front of it. Behind a reverse proxy, RemoteAddr
is the proxy's IP, not the client's — use [ClientIPFromHeader] or
[ClientIPFromXFF] instead.

IPv4 clients on a dual-stack listener surface as ::ffff:a.b.c.d; they
fold to plain v4 before storage so one logical client maps to one key.
IPv6 zones are preserved (link-local connections may legitimately have one).

funcs.name: ClientIPFromXFF
funcs.doc:
ClientIPFromXFF stores the client IP read from the X-Forwarded-For header,
walking the chain right-to-left and skipping any IP that falls within one
of the given trusted CIDR prefixes. The first IP that is not trusted is
the client. Read it with [GetClientIP].

An unparseable entry mid-chain aborts the walk and leaves no client IP
set (fail-closed) — we can't safely trust anything left of garbage.

Use this when you sit behind one or more reverse proxies whose IP ranges
you can enumerate as CIDRs:

	r.Use(middleware.ClientIPFromXFF(
	    "13.32.0.0/15",   // CloudFront IPv4
	    "52.46.0.0/18",   // CloudFront IPv4
	    "2600:9000::/28", // CloudFront IPv6
	))

Calling with no arguments returns the rightmost XFF entry, or no IP if
that entry doesn't parse (fail-closed) — safe only if you have exactly
one trusted hop directly in front of this server (e.g., nginx on localhost).

v4-mapped IPv6 (::ffff:a.b.c.d) folds to plain v4 and IPv6 zones are
stripped before the prefix check and storage; otherwise an attacker
could use either notation to alias a trusted IP past the check.

If you know the number of trusted proxies but not their IPs, use
[ClientIPFromXFFTrustedProxies] instead.

Panics at startup if any prefix is invalid.

funcs.name: ClientIPFromXFFTrustedProxies
funcs.doc:
ClientIPFromXFFTrustedProxies stores the client IP read from the
X-Forwarded-For header, given the exact number of trusted reverse proxies
between this server and the public internet. It returns the IP at position
len(xff) - numTrustedProxies in the merged X-Forwarded-For list — the IP
added by the outermost of your trusted proxies, the only IP in the chain
that none of your proxies have allowed an attacker to forge. Read it with
[GetClientIP].

Use this when:
  - You know exactly how many proxies you sit behind, AND
  - Their IP addresses are dynamic (autoscaling proxy pools, ephemeral
    containers, dynamic CDN edges) so listing CIDRs with [ClientIPFromXFF]
    is impractical.

WARNING: This variant is brittle to network architecture changes. If you
add or remove a proxy level, numTrustedProxies silently becomes wrong and
you may start trusting an attacker-supplied IP. Prefer [ClientIPFromXFF]
with explicit trusted CIDRs whenever you can.

If the XFF chain has fewer than numTrustedProxies entries (header missing
or architecture changed), no client IP is set and [GetClientIP] returns "".

Like [ClientIPFromXFF], v4-mapped IPv6 folds to plain v4 and IPv6 zones
are stripped before storage.

Panics at startup if numTrustedProxies < 1.

funcs.name: Compress
funcs.doc:
Compress is a middleware that compresses response
body of a given content types to a data format based
on Accept-Encoding request header. It uses a given
compression level.

NOTE: make sure to set the Content-Type header on your response
otherwise this middleware will not compress the response body. For ex, in
your handler you should set w.Header().Set("Content-Type", http.DetectContentType(yourBody))
or set it manually.

Passing a compression level of 5 is sensible value
funcs.signature: func Compress(level int, types ...string) func(next http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: ContentCharset
funcs.doc:
ContentCharset generates a handler that writes a 415 Unsupported Media Type response if none of the charsets match.
An empty charset will allow requests with no Content-Type header or no specified charset.

funcs.name: GetClientIP
funcs.doc:
GetClientIP returns the client IP as a string, as set by one of the
ClientIPFrom* middlewares. Returns "" if no valid IP was set.
Convenient for logging, rate-limit keys, etc.

funcs.name: GetClientIPAddr
funcs.doc:
GetClientIPAddr returns the client IP as a [netip.Addr], as set by one of
the ClientIPFrom* middlewares. The returned Addr is the zero value if not
set; use [netip.Addr.IsValid] to check. Useful when you need typed work —
prefix containment, Is4/Is6, etc. — without re-parsing the string.

funcs.name: GetHead
funcs.doc: GetHead automatically route undefined HEAD requests to GET handlers.

funcs.name: GetLogEntry
funcs.doc: GetLogEntry returns the in-context LogEntry for a request.

funcs.name: GetReqID
funcs.doc:
GetReqID returns a request ID from the given context if one is present.
Returns the empty string if a request ID cannot be found.

funcs.name: Heartbeat
funcs.doc:
Heartbeat endpoint middleware useful to setting up a path like
`/ping` that load balancers or uptime testing external services
can make a request before hitting any routes. It's also convenient
to place this above ACL middlewares as well.

funcs.name: Logger
funcs.doc:
Logger is a middleware that logs the start and end of each request, along
with some useful data about what was requested, what the response status was,
and how long it took to return. When standard output is a TTY, Logger will
print in color, otherwise it will print in black and white. Logger prints a
request ID if one is provided.

Alternatively, look at https://github.com/goware/httplog for a more in-depth
http logger with structured logging support.

IMPORTANT NOTE: Logger should go before any other middleware that may change
the response, such as middleware.Recoverer. Example:

	r := chi.NewRouter()
	r.Use(middleware.Logger)        // <--<< Logger should come before Recoverer
	r.Use(middleware.Recoverer)
	r.Get("/", handler)

funcs.name: Maybe
funcs.doc:
Maybe middleware will allow you to change the flow of the middleware stack execution depending on return
value of maybeFn(request). This is useful for example if you'd like to skip a middleware handler if
a request does not satisfy the maybeFn logic.
funcs.signature: func Maybe(mw func(http.Handler) http.Handler, maybeFn func(r *http.Request) bool) func(http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: New
funcs.doc: New will create a new middleware handler from a http.Handler.
funcs.signature: func New(h http.Handler) func(next http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: NewCompressor
funcs.doc:
NewCompressor creates a new Compressor that will handle encoding responses.

The level should be one of the ones defined in the flate package.
The types are the content types that are allowed to be compressed.

funcs.name: NewWrapResponseWriter
funcs.doc:
NewWrapResponseWriter wraps an http.ResponseWriter, returning a proxy that allows you to
hook into various parts of the response process.
funcs.signature: func NewWrapResponseWriter(w http.ResponseWriter, protoMajor int) WrapResponseWriter

funcs.name: NextRequestID
funcs.doc: NextRequestID generates the next request ID in the sequence.

funcs.name: NoCache
funcs.doc:
NoCache is a simple piece of middleware that sets a number of HTTP headers to prevent
a router (or subrouter) from being cached by an upstream proxy and/or client.

As per http://wiki.nginx.org/HttpProxyModule - NoCache sets:

	Expires: Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 UTC
	Cache-Control: no-cache, private, max-age=0
	X-Accel-Expires: 0
	Pragma: no-cache (for HTTP/1.0 proxies/clients)

funcs.name: PageRoute
funcs.doc:
PageRoute is a simple middleware which allows you to route a static GET request
at the middleware stack level.
funcs.signature: func PageRoute(path string, handler http.Handler) func(http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: PathRewrite
funcs.doc: PathRewrite is a simple middleware which allows you to rewrite the request URL path.
funcs.signature: func PathRewrite(old, new string) func(http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: Profiler
RealIP
funcs.doc:
RealIP is a middleware that sets a http.Request's RemoteAddr to the results
of parsing either the True-Client-IP, X-Real-IP or the X-Forwarded-For headers
(in that order).

Deprecated: RealIP is vulnerable to IP spoofing — it mutates r.RemoteAddr
to the leftmost X-Forwarded-For value, or to True-Client-IP / X-Real-IP
whether or not your infrastructure actually sets them. See
GHSA-3fxj-6jh8-hvhx, GHSA-rjr7-jggh-pgcp, GHSA-9g5q-2w5x-hmxf.

Use [ClientIPFromHeader], [ClientIPFromXFF], [ClientIPFromXFFTrustedProxies]
or [ClientIPFromRemoteAddr] and read the IP with [GetClientIP] instead.
These never mutate r.RemoteAddr.

funcs.name: Recoverer
funcs.doc:
Recoverer is a middleware that recovers from panics, logs the panic (and a
backtrace), and returns a HTTP 500 (Internal Server Error) status if
possible. Recoverer prints a request ID if one is provided.

Alternatively, look at https://github.com/go-chi/httplog middleware pkgs.

funcs.name: RedirectSlashes
funcs.doc:
RedirectSlashes is a middleware that will match request paths with a trailing
slash and redirect to the same path, less the trailing slash.

NOTE: RedirectSlashes middleware is *incompatible* with http.FileServer,
see https://github.com/go-chi/chi/issues/343

funcs.name: RequestID
funcs.doc:
RequestID is a middleware that injects a request ID into the context of each
request. A request ID is a string of the form "host.example.com/random-0001",
where "random" is a base62 random string that uniquely identifies this go
process, and where the last number is an atomically incremented request
counter.

funcs.name: RequestLogger
funcs.doc: RequestLogger returns a logger handler using a custom LogFormatter.
funcs.signature: func RequestLogger(f LogFormatter) func(next http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: RequestSize
funcs.doc:
RequestSize is a middleware that will limit request sizes to a specified
number of bytes. It uses MaxBytesReader to do so.

funcs.name: RouteHeaders
funcs.doc:
RouteHeaders is a neat little header-based router that allows you to direct
the flow of a request through a middleware stack based on a request header.

For example, lets say you'd like to setup multiple routers depending on the
request Host header, you could then do something as so:

	r := chi.NewRouter()
	rSubdomain := chi.NewRouter()
	r.Use(middleware.RouteHeaders().
		Route("Host", "example.com", middleware.New(r)).
		Route("Host", "*.example.com", middleware.New(rSubdomain)).
		Handler)
	r.Get("/", h)
	rSubdomain.Get("/", h2)

Another example, imagine you want to setup multiple CORS handlers, where for
your origin servers you allow authorized requests, but for third-party public
requests, authorization is disabled.

	r := chi.NewRouter()
	r.Use(middleware.RouteHeaders().
		Route("Origin", "https://app.skyweaver.net", cors.Handler(cors.Options{
			AllowedOrigins:   []string{"https://api.skyweaver.net"},
			AllowedMethods:   []string{"GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS"},
			AllowedHeaders:   []string{"Accept", "Authorization", "Content-Type"},
			AllowCredentials: true, // <----------<<< allow credentials
		})).
		Route("Origin", "*", cors.Handler(cors.Options{
			AllowedOrigins:   []string{"*"},
			AllowedMethods:   []string{"GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS"},
			AllowedHeaders:   []string{"Accept", "Content-Type"},
			AllowCredentials: false, // <----------<<< do not allow credentials
		})).
		Handler)

funcs.name: SetHeader
funcs.doc: SetHeader is a convenience handler to set a response header key/value
funcs.signature: func SetHeader(key, value string) func(http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: StripPrefix
funcs.doc:
StripPrefix is a middleware that will strip the provided prefix from the
request path before handing the request over to the next handler.

funcs.name: StripSlashes
funcs.doc:
StripSlashes is a middleware that will match request paths with a trailing
slash, strip it from the path and continue routing through the mux, if a route
matches, then it will serve the handler.

funcs.name: Sunset
funcs.doc:
Sunset set Deprecation/Sunset header to response
This can be used to enable Sunset in a route or a route group
For more: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8594.html
funcs.signature: func Sunset(sunsetAt time.Time, links ...string) func(http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: SupressNotFound
funcs.doc:
SupressNotFound will quickly respond with a 404 if the route is not found
and will not continue to the next middleware handler.

This is handy to put at the top of your middleware stack to avoid unnecessary
processing of requests that are not going to match any routes anyway. For
example its super annoying to see a bunch of 404's in your logs from bots.

funcs.name: Throttle
funcs.doc:
Throttle is a middleware that limits number of currently processed requests
at a time across all users. Note: Throttle is not a rate-limiter per user,
instead it just puts a ceiling on the number of current in-flight requests
being processed from the point from where the Throttle middleware is mounted.

funcs.name: ThrottleBacklog
funcs.doc:
ThrottleBacklog is a middleware that limits number of currently processed
requests at a time and provides a backlog for holding a finite number of
pending requests.
funcs.signature: func ThrottleBacklog(limit, backlogLimit int, backlogTimeout time.Duration) func(http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: ThrottleWithOpts
funcs.doc: ThrottleWithOpts is a middleware that limits number of currently processed requests using passed ThrottleOpts.

funcs.name: Timeout
funcs.doc:
Timeout is a middleware that cancels ctx after a given timeout and return
a 504 Gateway Timeout error to the client.

It's required that you select the ctx.Done() channel to check for the signal
if the context has reached its deadline and return, otherwise the timeout
signal will be just ignored.

ie. a route/handler may look like:

	r.Get("/long", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
		ctx := r.Context()
		processTime := time.Duration(rand.Intn(4)+1) * time.Second

		select {
		case <-ctx.Done():
			return

		case <-time.After(processTime):
			// The above channel simulates some hard work.
		}

		w.Write([]byte("done"))
	})
funcs.signature: func Timeout(timeout time.Duration) func(next http.Handler) http.Handler

funcs.name: URLFormat
funcs.doc:
URLFormat is a middleware that parses the url extension from a request path and stores it
on the context as a string under the key `middleware.URLFormatCtxKey`. The middleware will
trim the suffix from the routing path and continue routing.

Routers should not include a url parameter for the suffix when using this middleware.

Sample usage for url paths `/articles/1`, `/articles/1.json` and `/articles/1.xml`:

	func routes() http.Handler {
		r := chi.NewRouter()
		r.Use(middleware.URLFormat)

		r.Get("/articles/{id}", ListArticles)

		return r
	}

	func ListArticles(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
		urlFormat, _ := r.Context().Value(middleware.URLFormatCtxKey).(string)

		switch urlFormat {
		case "json":
			render.JSON(w, r, articles)
		case "xml:"
			render.XML(w, r, articles)
		default:
			render.JSON(w, r, articles)
		}
	}

funcs.name: WithLogEntry
funcs.doc: WithLogEntry sets the in-context LogEntry for a request.

funcs.name: WithValue
funcs.doc: WithValue is a middleware that sets a given key/value in a context chain.
funcs.signature: func WithValue(key, val interface{}) func(next http.Handler) http.Handler

types.name: Compressor
types.doc: Compressor represents a set of encoding configurations.

methods.name: Handler
methods.doc:
Handler returns a new middleware that will compress the response based on the
current Compressor.

methods.name: SetEncoder
methods.doc:
SetEncoder can be used to set the implementation of a compression algorithm.

The encoding should be a standardised identifier. See:
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Accept-Encoding

For example, add the Brotli algorithm:

	import brotli_enc "gopkg.in/kothar/brotli-go.v0/enc"

	compressor := middleware.NewCompressor(5, "text/html")
	compressor.SetEncoder("br", func(w io.Writer, level int) io.Writer {
		params := brotli_enc.NewBrotliParams()
		params.SetQuality(level)
		return brotli_enc.NewBrotliWriter(params, w)
	})

types.name: DefaultLogFormatter
types.doc: DefaultLogFormatter is a simple logger that implements a LogFormatter.

methods.name: NewLogEntry
methods.doc: NewLogEntry creates a new LogEntry for the request.

types.name: EncoderFunc
types.doc:
An EncoderFunc is a function that wraps the provided io.Writer with a
streaming compression algorithm and returns it.

In case of failure, the function should return nil.
types.declaration: type EncoderFunc func(w io.Writer, level int) io.Writer

types.name: HeaderRoute
HeaderRouter
types.declaration: type HeaderRouter map[string][]HeaderRoute

methods.name: Route
methods.signature: func  Router) Route(header, match string, middlewareHandler func(next http.Handler) http.Handler) HeaderRouter

methods.name: RouteAny
methods.signature: func  RouteAny(header string, match []string, middlewareHandler func(next http.Handler) http.Handler) HeaderRouter

methods.name: RouteDefault
methods.signature: func  RouteDefault(handler func(next http.Handler) http.Handler) HeaderRouter

types.name: LogEntry
types.doc:
LogEntry records the final log when a request completes.
See defaultLogEntry for an example implementation.

fields.name: Write
fields.type: func(status, bytes int, header http.Header, elapsed time.Duration, extra interface{})

types.name: LogFormatter
types.doc:
LogFormatter initiates the beginning of a new LogEntry per request.
See DefaultLogFormatter for an example implementation.

types.name: LoggerInterface
types.doc: LoggerInterface accepts printing to stdlib logger or compatible logger.

types.name: Pattern
ThrottleOpts
types.doc: ThrottleOpts represents a set of throttling options.
types.declaration:
type ThrottleOpts struct {
	RetryAfterFn   func(ctxDone bool) time.Duration
	Limit          int
	BacklogLimit   int
	BacklogTimeout time.Duration
	StatusCode     int
}

types.name: WrapResponseWriter
types.doc:
WrapResponseWriter is a proxy around an http.ResponseWriter that allows you to hook
into various parts of the response process.

fields.name: Status
fields.doc:
Status returns the HTTP status of the request, or 0 if one has not
yet been sent.

fields.name: BytesWritten
fields.doc: BytesWritten returns the total number of bytes sent to the client.

fields.name: Tee
fields.doc:
Tee causes the response body to be written to the given io.Writer in
addition to proxying the writes through. Only one io.Writer can be
tee'd to at once: setting a second one will overwrite the first.
Writes will be sent to the proxy before being written to this
io.Writer. It is illegal for the tee'd writer to be modified
concurrently with writes.

fields.name: Unwrap
fields.doc: Unwrap returns the original proxied target.

fields.name: Discard
fields.doc:
Discard causes all writes to the original ResponseWriter be discarded,
instead writing only to the tee'd writer if it's set.
The caller is responsible for calling WriteHeader and Write on the
original ResponseWriter once the processing is done.
